nativeRange.habitat: Reef-associated; typically encountered on reef flats and in lagoon and seaward reef areas at depths of roughly 0-40 m. Generally shelters during the day in dark crevices, under ledges, among branching corals, and notably among the long spines of the urchin Diadema setosum, emerging at dusk to forage.

diet.notes: Strictly carnivorous. Generally accepts a varied diet of meaty foods such as mysid shrimp, vitamin-enriched brine shrimp, finely chopped marine flesh, bloodworms, and quality marine pellets or flakes once settled. Evening or dusk feeding tends to improve acceptance given the species' nocturnal foraging tendency. Larger specimens have been reported to take small live feeder shrimp and bite-sized fish.

summary: A predatory Indo-Pacific cardinalfish (to ~13 cm) with five black horizontal stripes and a yellow caudal spot. Generally hardy and beginner-friendly, but nocturnal and will eat small ornamental shrimp and bite-sized tankmates.
careNotes:
The Five Lined Cardinalfish is one of the larger commonly imported cardinalfishes, distinguished by five sharp black horizontal stripes running along a silvery body and a small yellow spot at the base of the caudal fin. It is widely distributed across the Indo-Pacific from the Red Sea and East Africa east to the Pitcairn Group, and north to southern Japan. In the wild it occurs singly or in loose aggregations on reef flats and seaward reefs, often sheltering during the day in dark crevices, under ledges, among branching corals, or notably among the long defensive spines of the urchin Diadema setosum.

The species is largely nocturnal and tends to rest in shaded rockwork during the day, emerging at dusk to hunt small crustaceans, gastropods, and small fishes. In captivity this generally translates to a recommendation for plenty of caves, ledges, and shaded retreats, and evening feeding for newly added specimens. Most sources describe it as a slow and methodical swimmer that does not bother similarly sized or larger tankmates and is generally easy to keep.

Reef-safety is the main cross-source disagreement. LiveAquaria lists it as reef-compatible; Quality Marine and Aquatic Community both warn it will hunt small ornamental shrimp, very small fish, and feather dusters overnight. The value written here is 'with caution': it is safe with corals and sessile invertebrates, but should not be housed with bite-sized tankmates.

Social behaviour is also worth noting — sources report that small groups establish a loose hierarchy without overt aggression when given adequate space, but the species is sufficiently piscivorous that very small conspecifics or other tiny cardinalfishes may be eaten. A group of 3-5 individuals introduced together into a tank with ample rockwork tends to settle best. Maximum size reported by FishBase and Aquatic Community is 13 cm TL; LiveAquaria's 5 in maximum agrees.

Water parameters should sit in the typical tropical-marine reef range: temperature roughly 24-27°C (aquarium-hobby sources cite 22-26°C / 72-78°F; FishBase wild-range preferred is 25-29°C), pH 8.1-8.4, specific gravity 1.020-1.025, and alkalinity around 8-12 dKH. Lifespan was not stated by the sources reviewed.

Tank-size note: LiveAquaria and Quality Marine give a 50 US gal (~190 L) minimum; for a small group of this size-class cardinalfish in a 6-ft footprint, biasing to 200 L (with at least a 120 cm length) tends to better accommodate the species' need for both rockwork retreats and open swimming space.
breedingNotes: Paternal mouthbrooder, like other apogonids. The species shows distinct pair courtship and spawning; the male carries the egg mass in his buccal cavity for roughly 1-3 weeks and largely ceases feeding during brooding (a visibly swollen jaw is the most obvious sign). Has been successfully reared in captivity according to FishBase, though dedicated breeding setups generally improve fry survival as larvae are pelagic and vulnerable to predation in community tanks.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary husbandry source — gave scientific name (Cheilodipterus quinquelineatus), family (Apogonidae), care level (easy), temperament (semi-aggressive), minimum tank size (50 gal, 6-ft tank recommended for groups), reef-compatible (yes), diet (meaty carnivore), max size 5 in, water parameters 72-78°F / pH 8.1-8.4 / SG 1.020-1.025 / dKH 8-12, hides in seagrass / among urchin spines, slow methodical swimmer, hierarchy without aggression when space permits.

additional.name: Reef2Reef wiki
additional.notes: Designated secondary returned HTTP 403 Forbidden and could not be fetched; fell back to FishBase, Quality Marine, and Aquatic Community for cross-verification.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Taxonomy authority: Apogonidae, Kurtiformes; max size 13.0 cm TL; depth 0-40 m; Indo-Pacific distribution (Red Sea to Pitcairn Group, southern Japan to Lord Howe/Rapa); preferred wild temperature 24.9-29°C (mean 27.9); shelters in dark crevices, branched coral, ledges, and among Diadema setosum spines; nocturnal; diet of small crustaceans, gastropods, small fishes; mouthbrooder with distinct pair courtship; has been reared in captivity.

additional.name: Quality Marine
additional.notes: Cross-checked husbandry: 50 gal min, 72-78°F, pH 8.1-8.4, SG 1.020-1.025, dKH 8-12, ~5 in max, nocturnal (active at night, rests during day), schooling. Disagrees with LiveAquaria on reef-safety: explicitly warns NOT to house with smaller fish, shrimp, or feather dusters. Final written value 'with caution' reflects this spread. Notes 'some Cardinalfish are mouth brooders'.

additional.name: Aquatic Community
additional.notes: Confirmed max size 13.0 cm / 5.2 in, temperature 72-78°F, Indo-Pacific distribution, and nocturnal habit (hides during day, needs dark shelter). Notes the species feeds on small fishes, shrimp, and meaty foods, and that the species shouldn't be kept with larger predators. Breeding not documented in aquaria per this source.

fish.breedingNotes: Paternal mouthbrooder with distinct pair courtship; male carries egg mass in buccal cavity for roughly 1-3 weeks and stops feeding during brooding. FishBase notes the species has been reared in captivity; routine captive breeding remains uncommon in the hobby.
